questions.question: Let f be a function from a set A to a set B, g a function from B to C, and h a function from A to C, such that h(a) = g(f(a)) for all a in A. Which of the following statements is always true for all such functions f and g? A. g is onto => h is onto  B. h is onto => f is onto  C. h is onto => g is onto  D. h is onto => f and g are onto
questions.answer: C. h is onto => g is onto
questions.to_find: Which implication about onto-ness always holds for the composition h = g . f.

understand.plain: Here h is the composition g . f: you first apply f (taking A into B) and then apply g (taking B into C). 'Onto' (surjective) means the function reaches every element of its codomain. The key fact is that the image of a composition can only be as large as the image of the outer map g - so if the whole thing h reaches all of C, then g certainly must reach all of C too. But h reaching all of C tells you nothing about whether f covers all of B, because g could collapse a small part of B onto all of C.

keywords.explain: the rule h(a) = g(f(a)): apply f first, then feed the result into g
keywords.example: if f(a)=b and g(b)=c then h(a)=c

keywords.explain: a function is onto when every element of its codomain is hit by at least one input
keywords.example: g: B->C is onto if every c in C equals g(b) for some b

keywords.explain: range(g . f) is a subset of range(g), so the composition can never reach more of C than g alone does
keywords.example: if g misses some c in C, then h = g . f must miss it too

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Composition h = g composed with f from A to B to C'><title>h = g . f : A -> B -> C</title><desc>f maps A into B and g maps B into C; if h = g . f covers all of C then g must cover all of C, but f need not cover all of B.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx='90' cy='130' rx='55' ry='80' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><ellipse cx='310' cy='130' rx='55' ry='80' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><ellipse cx='530' cy='130' rx='55' ry='80' class='bg'/><text x='530' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><circle cx='90' cy='110' r='5' class='ink'/><circle cx='90' cy='160' r='5' class='ink'/><circle cx='310' cy='95' r='5' class='ink'/><circle cx='310' cy='140' r='5' class='ink'/><circle cx='310' cy='180' r='5' class='mut'/><circle cx='530' cy='110' r='5' class='ink'/><circle cx='530' cy='160' r='5' class='ink'/><line x1='95' y1='110' x2='305' y2='95' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='95' y1='160' x2='305' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='315' y1='95' x2='525' y2='110' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='315' y1='140' x2='525' y2='160' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='315' y1='180' x2='525' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='200' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>f (may miss the grey point in B)</text><text x='460' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>g hits all of C</text><text x='310' y='268' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>h onto C  =>  g onto C  (f need not be onto B)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three blobs A, B, C with arrows f from A to B and g from B to C; the composition h reaches all of C, forcing g to reach all of C, while one point of B is never used by f.

given.aim: Determine which onto implication is guaranteed for every choice of f and g with h = g . f.

terms.meaning: the inner function applied first
terms.example: f sends each a in A to some b in B
terms.connects: its image is the input region that g then acts on

terms.meaning: the outer function applied to f's output
terms.example: g sends b to g(b) in C
terms.connects: g is the only map whose codomain is C, so it controls coverage of C

terms.meaning: the composition with h(a) = g(f(a))
terms.example: h onto means every c in C is some g(f(a))
terms.connects: range(h) is a subset of range(g)

terms.meaning: covering every element of the target set C
terms.example: h onto C requires C = range(h)
terms.connects: C = range(h) subset of range(g) forces range(g) = C

given.plan: Use range(g . f) subset of range(g): if h is onto C then range(g) must equal C, proving g is onto; then give a counterexample showing f need not be onto.

steps.title: Show h onto forces g onto
steps.formula_raw: range(g . f) subset of range(g); if range(h)=C then range(g)=C
steps.apply: h onto means every c in C equals h(a) = g(f(a)) for some a, so c = g(b) with b = f(a) in B; thus every c in C is an image under g, i.e. g is onto
steps.note: the inner map f cannot enlarge what g can reach, so coverage of C rests entirely on g

steps.title: Rule out the other options by counterexample
steps.formula_raw: f need not be onto even when h is onto
steps.apply: take A={1}, B={x,y}, C={c} with f(1)=x and g(x)=g(y)=c; then h(1)=c is onto C, yet f misses y so f is not onto, killing B and D; option A fails since g onto does not force h to reach C when f's image is too small
steps.note: only option C survives, matching the surjectivity of the outer map

solution.result: h is onto => g is onto (Option C)

questions.formula_note: Because range(g . f) is a subset of range(g), h onto C forces g onto C, so option C is the only universally true statement.

questions.to_find: Which of the four boolean identities fails to hold in general.

understand.plain: Options A, B, and C are all forms of Shannon's expansion, which splits a function around one variable using that variable and its complement as 'selectors'. In A and B the function is multiplied by x and x' and added back, which simply rebuilds f because x + x' = 1. C is the genuine expansion about x_n, with the cofactors f(...,0) and f(...,1) gated by x_n' and x_n. Option D drops the gating literals entirely and just ORs the two cofactors about x_1 - that is wrong, because OR-ing the two halves loses the selection and can turn a 0 into a 1.

keywords.explain: f = x_i' f|_{x_i=0} + x_i f|_{x_i=1}; the complement and the variable select the correct cofactor
f|_{x_i=0} and f|_{x_i=1} are f with that variable forced to 0 or 1
keywords.example: f(0, x_2, ..., x_n) is the x_1 = 0 cofactor

keywords.explain: a literal OR its complement is always 1, which is why x f + x' f = f

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 270' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Shannon expansion versus the wrong OR of cofactors'><title>Correct Shannon expansion vs option D</title><desc>Options A, B, C correctly select a cofactor with x' and x; option D ORs both cofactors without the gating literals, which is not equal to f in general.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='25' y='40' width='270' height='90' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='160' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>correct (A, B, C)</text><text x='160' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>f = x' f|0 + x f|1</text><rect x='325' y='40' width='270' height='90' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='460' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>option D (wrong)</text><text x='460' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl no'>f = f|0 + f|1</text><text x='310' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>counterexample: f = x_1 (n=1)</text><text x='310' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>D gives f(0) + f(1) = 0 + 1 = 1 for all x_1</text><text x='310' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>but f = x_1 is 0 when x_1 = 0  =>  D fails</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes contrast the correct Shannon expansion f = x' f|0 + x f|1 with option D's f = f|0 + f|1, plus the counterexample f = x_1 where D wrongly yields constant 1.

given.aim: Identify the equation that is not a valid boolean identity for every f.

terms.meaning: an arbitrary boolean (switching) function of n variables
terms.example: f could be x_1 alone, or x_1 AND x_2
terms.connects: the object each identity claims to rewrite

terms.meaning: the NOT of variable x_i, used as a selector in the expansion
terms.example: x_1' is 1 exactly when x_1 = 0
terms.connects: gates the x_i = 0 cofactor in correct expansions

terms.meaning: cofactors obtained by fixing one variable to 0 or 1
terms.connects: must be gated by x_i' and x_i, not merely OR-ed

terms.meaning: boolean disjunction used to recombine the two cofactors
terms.connects: without selectors, OR-ing both cofactors overcounts and breaks equality

given.plan: Recognise A, B, C as valid Shannon-expansion forms (using x + x' = 1 or proper gating), then expose D as missing the selector literals via a one-variable counterexample.

steps.title: Verify A, B, C are valid expansions
steps.apply: A and B have the form x f + x' f = (x + x')f = f using the complement law; C is the exact Shannon expansion about x_n with cofactors f(...,0), f(...,1) gated by x_n' and x_n, so all three are always true
steps.note: the gating literals x_i' and x_i are what make the two cofactors select correctly

steps.title: Expose D with a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: f(0, x_2, ..., x_n) + f(1, x_2, ..., x_n)  has no x_1 / x_1' selectors
steps.apply: take n = 1 and f = x_1; then D claims f = f(0) + f(1) = 0 + 1 = 1 for every x_1, but f(0) = 0, so D fails. The correct identity needs x_1' f(0,...) + x_1 f(1,...)
steps.note: OR-ing both cofactors without the gating literals is not equal to f in general

solution.result: Option D is NOT true; it omits the selecting literals x_1' and x_1 of Shannon's expansion.

questions.formula_note: Shannon's expansion gates each cofactor with x_i' and x_i; option D simply ORs the cofactors of x_1 without those selectors, so it is the false equation.

questions.question: Show that if G is a group such that (a * b)^2 = a^2 * b^2 for all a, b belonging to G, then G is abelian.
questions.answer: N/A - Proof: From (a*b)^2 = a^2*b^2 we get a*b*a*b = a*a*b*b; left-cancel a and right-cancel b to obtain b*a = a*b for all a,b, hence G is abelian.
questions.to_find: Prove G is commutative (abelian) given (a*b)^2 = a^2*b^2 for all a, b.

understand.plain: In any group the squaring shortcut (a*b)^2 = a^2*b^2 is special - it normally fails because elements need not commute. The trick is to write out both sides as products of four factors and then use the group's cancellation laws (every element has an inverse). After cancelling one a from the front and one b from the back, the two middle factors are forced to be equal in either order, which is exactly the commutative law a*b = b*a.

keywords.explain: a group whose operation is commutative: a*b = b*a for all elements
keywords.example: the integers under addition are abelian

keywords.explain: in a group, a*x = a*y implies x = y (left), and x*a = y*a implies x = y (right), because inverses exist
keywords.example: from a*b*a*b = a*a*b*b cancel the leading a to get b*a*b = a*b*b

keywords.explain: grouping does not matter, so (a*b)^2 = a*b*a*b can be regrouped freely

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Cancellation reduces the squaring identity to commutativity'><title>From (a*b)^2 = a^2*b^2 to a*b = b*a</title><desc>Expanding both sides gives a b a b = a a b b; cancelling a on the left and b on the right leaves b a = a b, the commutative law.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='40' width='540' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a*b*a*b  =  a*a*b*b</text><rect x='40' y='100' width='540' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='310' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>cancel leading a  =>  b*a*b = a*b*b</text><rect x='40' y='160' width='540' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='310' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>cancel trailing b  =>  b*a = a*b</text><text x='310' y='238' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>a*b = b*a for all a,b  =>  G is abelian</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A three-row reduction: a b a b = a a b b, then cancel a on the left, then cancel b on the right to reach b a = a b, proving G is abelian.

given.aim: Prove the operation of G is commutative under the hypothesis (a*b)^2 = a^2*b^2 for all a, b.

terms.meaning: a group: closed, associative, with identity and inverses
terms.example: inverses give the cancellation laws we rely on
terms.connects: the structure whose commutativity we must establish

terms.meaning: the element a*b composed with itself, equal to a*b*a*b by associativity
terms.connects: the left-hand side of the hypothesis

terms.meaning: a*a*b*b, the right-hand side of the hypothesis
terms.example: a^2 = a*a and b^2 = b*b
terms.connects: set equal to (a*b)^2 by assumption

terms.meaning: the group elements that enable cancellation
terms.example: multiply by a^{-1} on the left to cancel a
terms.connects: turn the four-factor equation into b*a = a*b

given.plan: Expand both sides into four factors, equate them, then cancel a on the left and b on the right using the group cancellation laws to reach a*b = b*a.

steps.title: Expand both sides of the hypothesis
steps.apply: by associativity (a*b)^2 = (a*b)*(a*b) = a*b*a*b; the hypothesis sets this equal to a^2*b^2 = a*a*b*b, giving a*b*a*b = a*a*b*b
steps.note: no commutativity is assumed yet - only associativity is used to drop the brackets

steps.title: Cancel a on the left and b on the right
steps.formula_raw: a*(b*a*b) = a*(a*b*b)  =>  b*a*b = a*b*b ; then (b*a)*b = (a*b)*b  =>  b*a = a*b
steps.apply: left-multiply by a^{-1} to cancel the leading a, getting b*a*b = a*b*b; then right-multiply by b^{-1} to cancel the trailing b, getting b*a = a*b for all a, b in G
steps.note: cancellation is valid in any group because every element has an inverse

solution.result: a*b = b*a holds for all a, b in G, so G is abelian.

questions.formula_note: Expanding (a*b)^2 = a^2*b^2 and applying the group cancellation laws (front a, back b) directly yields a*b = b*a, the abelian property.

questions.question: Match the pairs in the following. (a) Groups  (b) Semigroups  (c) Monoids  (d) Abelian groups   with   (p) Associativity  (q) Identity  (r) Commutativity  (s) Left inverse
questions.answer: a-s, b-p, c-q, d-r  (Groups - Left inverse, Semigroups - Associativity, Monoids - Identity, Abelian groups - Commutativity)
questions.to_find: Match each algebraic structure with the defining property it adds.

understand.plain: These four structures form a ladder, each adding one new requirement on top of the previous. A set with an associative binary operation is a semigroup. Add an identity element and it becomes a monoid. Add inverses (a left inverse for every element) and it becomes a group. Add commutativity and the group becomes abelian. So the match pairs each structure with the extra property that distinguishes it: semigroup with associativity, monoid with identity, group with (left) inverse, abelian group with commutativity.

keywords.explain: a set with a binary operation that is associative - nothing more is required
keywords.example: positive integers under addition form a semigroup

keywords.explain: a semigroup that also has an identity element e with e*a = a*e = a
keywords.example: strings under concatenation with the empty string as identity

keywords.explain: a group is a monoid in which every element has an inverse; it is abelian if the operation is also commutative
keywords.example: integers under addition form an abelian group; invertible matrices under multiplication form a non-abelian group

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Hierarchy of algebraic structures'><title>Semigroup to monoid to group to abelian group</title><desc>Each structure adds one property: semigroup adds associativity, monoid adds identity, group adds inverse, abelian group adds commutativity.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='40' width='200' height='46' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='69' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(b) Semigroup</text><text x='420' y='69' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>(p) Associativity</text><rect x='40' y='110' width='200' height='46' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='139' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(c) Monoid</text><text x='420' y='139' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>(q) Identity</text><rect x='40' y='180' width='200' height='46' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='209' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(a) Group</text><text x='420' y='209' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>(s) Left inverse</text><rect x='40' y='250' width='200' height='46' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='140' y='279' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(d) Abelian group</text><text x='420' y='279' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>(r) Commutativity</text><line x1='245' y1='63' x2='300' y2='63' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='245' y1='133' x2='300' y2='133' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='245' y1='203' x2='300' y2='203' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='245' y1='273' x2='300' y2='273' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A ladder pairing semigroup with associativity, monoid with identity, group with left inverse, and abelian group with commutativity, each level adding one property.

given.aim: Pair (a) Groups, (b) Semigroups, (c) Monoids, (d) Abelian groups with the property that defines each.

terms.meaning: (a*b)*c = a*(b*c); the single axiom that defines a semigroup
terms.connects: matches (b) Semigroups

terms.meaning: an element e with e*a = a*e = a; what a monoid adds to a semigroup
terms.example: 0 for addition, 1 for multiplication
terms.connects: matches (c) Monoids

terms.meaning: for each a an element a^{-1} with a^{-1}*a = e; what a group adds to a monoid
terms.example: -3 is the inverse of 3 under addition
terms.connects: matches (a) Groups

terms.meaning: a*b = b*a; what an abelian group adds to a group
terms.connects: matches (d) Abelian groups

given.plan: Walk up the structure hierarchy and pair each structure with the one extra defining property: semigroup-associativity, monoid-identity, group-inverse, abelian group-commutativity.

steps.title: Identify the defining property added at each level
steps.formula_raw: semigroup = associativity; monoid = + identity; group = + inverse; abelian = + commutativity
steps.apply: (b) Semigroups need only associativity -> (p); (c) Monoids add an identity -> (q); the distinguishing axiom for (a) Groups beyond a monoid is the existence of inverses -> left inverse (s)
steps.note: each structure is the previous one plus exactly one axiom

steps.title: Match the abelian group
steps.apply: (d) Abelian groups are groups whose operation is also commutative, so they match commutativity -> (r); the remaining property associativity is the semigroup's, identity the monoid's
steps.note: this leaves the clean one-to-one matching a-s, b-p, c-q, d-r

solution.result: a-s (Groups - Left inverse), b-p (Semigroups - Associativity), c-q (Monoids - Identity), d-r (Abelian groups - Commutativity)

questions.formula_note: The structures form a hierarchy where each adds one axiom; matching each with its distinguishing property gives a-s, b-p, c-q, d-r. The official key marks this match-the-following item as N/A (descriptive).

questions.question: If G is a group of even order, then show that there exists an element a != e, the identity in G, such that a^2 = e.
questions.answer: N/A - Proof: Pair each element with its inverse. Elements with a != a^{-1} occur in disjoint pairs (even count). Since |G| is even and e is self-inverse, the number of self-inverse elements is even, so at least one a != e satisfies a = a^{-1}, i.e. a^2 = e.
questions.to_find: Prove a group of even order contains a non-identity element of order 2.

understand.plain: An element of order 2 is one that is its own inverse but is not the identity, since a = a^{-1} is the same as a^2 = e. The clever counting idea is to pair up every element with its inverse. Elements that differ from their own inverse come in neat two-element pairs {a, a^{-1}}, so together they account for an even number of elements. Because the whole group has an even number of elements and the identity is always its own inverse, the count of self-inverse elements must be even - and since it is at least one (the identity), it is at least two, giving a non-identity self-inverse element.

keywords.explain: the number of elements in G; here it is even, written |G| = 2n
keywords.example: Z_4 (integers mod 4 under addition) has order 4

keywords.explain: an element a with a = a^{-1}, equivalently a^2 = e; the identity is always one
group elements with a != a^{-1} split into disjoint pairs {a, a^{-1}}, contributing an even count
keywords.example: in Z_4, 1 and 3 pair up since 1+3 = 0

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 270' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Pairing elements with their inverses in a group of even order'><title>Even order forces a non-identity involution</title><desc>Elements with a != a-inverse form disjoint pairs (even count); the identity is self-inverse, so an even total forces at least one more self-inverse element a with a^2 = e.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='310' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>|G| even  =>  pair a with a^{-1}</text><circle cx='120' cy='90' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a</text><circle cx='220' cy='90' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a^{-1}</text><line x1='142' y1='90' x2='198' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='360' cy='90' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b</text><circle cx='460' cy='90' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='460' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b^{-1}</text><line x1='382' y1='90' x2='438' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='300' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>pairs use an even number of elements</text><circle cx='200' cy='195' r='24' class='ok' opacity='0.25'/><text x='200' y='201' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e</text><circle cx='400' cy='195' r='24' class='hi'/><text x='400' y='201' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a, a^2=e</text><text x='310' y='255' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>even total => an extra self-inverse a != e exists, a^2 = e</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Disjoint pairs {a, a-inverse} use an even number of elements; with the identity self-inverse and the total even, at least one more self-inverse element a != e exists satisfying a^2 = e.

given.aim: Show every group of even order has an element a != e with a^2 = e.

terms.meaning: a finite group with |G| = 2n for some integer n >= 1
terms.example: the symmetry group of an equilateral triangle has order 6
terms.connects: evenness is what forces the extra involution

terms.meaning: the unique element with e*a = a*e = a; it satisfies e^{-1} = e
terms.connects: always one self-inverse element, so the count is at least 1

terms.meaning: the element with a*a^{-1} = e; inverses are unique
terms.example: in Z_4 the inverse of 1 is 3
terms.connects: lets us pair non-self-inverse elements

terms.meaning: the condition a = a^{-1}, defining a self-inverse element of order 1 or 2
terms.example: 2 in Z_4 satisfies 2+2 = 0
terms.connects: the property the required element a must have

given.plan: Partition G by pairing each element with its inverse; non-self-inverse elements use an even count, so evenness of |G| forces the self-inverse count to be even and hence to exceed the single identity.

steps.title: Pair non-self-inverse elements
steps.formula_raw: if a != a^{-1} then {a, a^{-1}} is a 2-element set, disjoint from other such pairs
steps.apply: elements with a != a^{-1} partition into disjoint unordered pairs {a, a^{-1}}; the total number of such elements is therefore even
steps.note: inverses are unique, so the pairing is well defined and non-overlapping

steps.title: Count self-inverse elements using even order
steps.apply: the self-inverse elements (a = a^{-1}) include e; their count equals |G| minus an even number, and since |G| is even the count is even; an even count that contains e must be at least 2, so some a != e has a = a^{-1}, i.e. a^2 = e
steps.note: this a has order exactly 2; the result is a corollary of Cauchy's theorem for the prime 2

solution.result: There exists a != e in G with a^2 = e (an element of order 2).

questions.formula_note: Pairing each element with its inverse shows non-self-inverse elements come in pairs; an even |G| then forces an even number of self-inverse elements, so beyond e at least one a != e has a^2 = e.

questions.question: Let ({p, q}, *) be a semigroup where p * p = q. Show that: a. p * q = q * p, and b. q * q = q.
questions.answer: N/A - Proof: (a) p*q = p*(p*p) = (p*p)*p = q*p by associativity. (b) q*q = (q*p)*p = (p*q)*p; since p*q is in {p,q}, if p*q=p then it equals p*p=q, and if p*q=q then it equals q*p=p*q=q; either way q*q = q.
questions.to_find: Prove p*q = q*p and q*q = q in the two-element semigroup with p*p = q.

understand.plain: A semigroup just means the operation * is associative (we get to regroup, but cannot assume commutativity or an identity). The set has only two elements {p, q}, and we are told p*p = q. Part (a) follows by sliding the brackets: p*q is p*(p*p), which regroups to (p*p)*p = q*p. Part (b) uses closure - q*q must be either p or q - together with associativity to pin it down to q in both possible cases.

keywords.explain: a set with an associative binary operation; no identity or inverses are assumed
(x*y)*z = x*(y*z); the only tool we may use beyond closure
the result of * stays inside the set, so p*q and q*q each equal p or q

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Two-element semigroup proof'><title>Semigroup with p*p = q</title><desc>Associativity gives p*q = p*(p*p) = (p*p)*p = q*p; then closure plus associativity force q*q = q in both cases for p*q.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='40' width='540' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='310' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(a)  p*q = p*(p*p) = (p*p)*p = q*p</text><rect x='40' y='100' width='540' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(b)  q*q = (p*p)*p ... using q = p*p and p*q in {p,q}</text><rect x='40' y='160' width='260' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='182' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if p*q = p:</text><text x='170' y='202' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>q*q = p*p = q</text><rect x='320' y='160' width='260' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='450' y='182' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if p*q = q:</text><text x='450' y='202' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>q*q = q*p = q</text><text x='310' y='255' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>both cases give q*q = q</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A flow showing p*q = q*p by associativity, then a case split on p*q in {p,q}, with both cases yielding q*q = q.

given.aim: Prove the two stated identities in the semigroup ({p,q}, *) given p*p = q.

terms.meaning: the underlying two-element set; every product lands back in it (closure)
terms.example: p*q is p or q
terms.connects: limits the cases we must check in part (b)

terms.meaning: the semigroup operation; (x*y)*z = x*(y*z) for all elements
terms.example: p*(p*p) = (p*p)*p
terms.connects: the engine of both proofs

terms.meaning: the given defining relation of this semigroup
terms.example: lets us replace p*p by q anywhere
terms.connects: substituted to introduce and remove q

terms.meaning: the target product, which we show equals q
terms.example: q*q = (p*p)*p after substitution and regrouping
terms.connects: resolved by a two-case argument on p*q

given.plan: For (a) regroup p*q = p*(p*p) = (p*p)*p = q*p. For (b) write q*q = (p*p)*p = (p*q)*... and split on the only two values p*q can take, showing each gives q.

steps.title: Prove p*q = q*p by associativity
steps.apply: substitute q = p*p into p*q to get p*(p*p); regroup by associativity to (p*p)*p; substitute back q = p*p to get q*p, so p*q = q*p
steps.note: commutativity is not assumed - it is derived here for these particular elements

steps.title: Prove q*q = q by closure and cases
steps.apply: q*q = (q*p)*p = (p*q)*p by part (a); closure forces p*q to be p or q. If p*q = p, then q*q = p*p = q. If p*q = q, then q*q = q*p = p*q = q. In both cases q*q = q
steps.note: the two-element closure makes the case analysis exhaustive

solution.result: p*q = q*p and q*q = q both hold in the semigroup.

questions.formula_note: Associativity alone gives p*q = p*(p*p) = (p*p)*p = q*p, and combining it with two-element closure forces q*q = q in every case.

formulas.name: Surjectivity of a Composition Implies the Outer Map is Surjective
formulas.plain: if the composition h = g . f is onto its codomain C, then the outer function g must be onto C, because range(g . f) is always a subset of range(g); the inner map f need not be onto
formulas.whenToUse: when reasoning about which factor of a composed function must be surjective given that the whole composition is surjective

terms.means: the outer function whose codomain is C

formulas.trap: the converse direction fails: h onto does NOT force f onto, since g can map a proper subset of B onto all of C; only the outer map's surjectivity is guaranteed
formulas.reference: Properties of function composition; surjections in set theory

formulas.name: Shannon's Expansion of a Boolean Function
formulas.plain: any boolean function can be expanded about a variable x_i as f = x_i' f(...,x_i=0,...) + x_i f(...,x_i=1,...); the literals x_i' and x_i act as selectors that pick the correct cofactor
formulas.whenToUse: to decompose, simplify, or verify identities of boolean functions about a chosen variable (used in BDDs and multiplexer-based logic)

terms.means: the splitting variable and its complement, used as selectors
the cofactors obtained by fixing x_i to 0 or 1

formulas.trap: you must keep the gating literals: f = f|0 + f|1 (without x_i' and x_i) is NOT valid in general, since OR-ing both cofactors loses the selection and can flip a 0 to a 1
formulas.reference: Boolean algebra; Shannon's expansion theorem in switching theory

formulas.name: Group Axioms
formulas.plain: a group (G, *) is a set with a binary operation that is closed and associative, has an identity element e (e*a = a*e = a), and gives every element a an inverse a^{-1} (a*a^{-1} = a^{-1}*a = e)
formulas.whenToUse: whenever proving general statements about groups, such as deriving commutativity or properties of elements from the defining axioms

terms.means: the associative binary operation of the group

formulas.trap: commutativity is NOT a group axiom; a group is abelian only if a*b = b*a is additionally true, so never assume it when proving a structure is abelian
formulas.reference: Definition of a group; abstract algebra

formulas.name: Cancellation Laws in a Group
formulas.latex: a*x = a*y \Rightarrow x = y; \quad x*a = y*a \Rightarrow x = y
formulas.plain: in any group you may cancel a common factor: left cancellation says a*x = a*y implies x = y, and right cancellation says x*a = y*a implies x = y, because every element has an inverse
formulas.whenToUse: to simplify group equations by removing a common element from the left or right side

terms.means: the element being cancelled (it must be invertible, true in any group)
the elements shown to be equal after cancellation

formulas.trap: cancellation only removes a factor from the same side on both sides; you cannot cancel a from the left of one product and the right of another, and it requires a true group (invertibility), not just a semigroup
formulas.reference: Cancellation laws follow from invertibility; abstract algebra

formulas.name: Hierarchy of Algebraic Structures
formulas.plain: the standard algebraic structures form a ladder where each adds one axiom: a semigroup has an associative operation, a monoid adds an identity, a group adds inverses, and an abelian group adds commutativity
formulas.whenToUse: to classify an algebraic structure or to recall which defining property distinguishes semigroups, monoids, groups, and abelian groups
formulas.trap: associativity is required at every level (it is the semigroup axiom); commutativity is the distinguishing extra of abelian groups only, not of ordinary groups
formulas.reference: Classification of algebraic structures; abstract algebra

formulas.name: Self-Inverse Element (Involution)
formulas.plain: an element a of a group is its own inverse exactly when a^2 = e; such an element has order 1 (the identity) or order 2; non-self-inverse elements pair up as {a, a^{-1}}, which is the basis of the even-order counting argument
formulas.whenToUse: when counting elements of order 2 or proving existence of an involution, e.g. in a group of even order

terms.means: the identity, which is always self-inverse
a disjoint pair of distinct mutually inverse elements

formulas.trap: the identity e always satisfies e^2 = e, so it is self-inverse but is excluded when seeking a != e; a group of even order is guaranteed at least one non-identity involution (a corollary of Cauchy's theorem for p = 2)
formulas.reference: Order of group elements; Cauchy's theorem corollary, abstract algebra

formulas.name: Semigroup Associativity
formulas.plain: a semigroup is a set with a binary operation * that is associative, meaning regrouping does not change the result; no identity, inverses, or commutativity may be assumed
formulas.whenToUse: to manipulate products in a semigroup by regrouping, the only structural law available beyond closure
formulas.trap: in a semigroup you may NOT assume commutativity or the existence of an identity/inverse; only associativity and closure are guaranteed, so cancellation is not generally valid
formulas.reference: Definition of a semigroup; abstract algebra
